memories.memory_text: I stared at Berlin in that empty room, and for a moment he wasn't him—he was the man I lost, standing in the same spot, breathless, silent, just like before the wind carried everything away.
grounded_facts: Helena observes Berlin standing in the attic apartment.
She notes his breathlessness and posture, which trigger a memory of the deceased man.
She recalls the deceased man being in the same position, with nothing happening except the wind.
She remembers the moment before leaving, when she said she would get her coat and wait downstairs.

memories.memory_text: I think that's when I heard her call him John—standing there, wanting to feel the snow on my face, and that name just surfaced, like it had been buried.
grounded_facts: HELENA recalls hearing someone call the suspect 'John' while she was on the fire escape.
She associates the memory with the sensation of wanting to feel snow on her face.

memories.memory_text: I asked if Amber was dead, and when Berlin said yes, the silence after felt heavier than before—like something final had settled.
grounded_facts: HELENA directly asks Berlin whether Amber is dead.
Berlin confirms Amber's death, and the resulting silence is noted as significant by HELENA.

memories.scene_title: 56 EXT. ROSS'S BOAT. HUMBOLDT BAY. EUREKA. DAY.
memories.memory_text: I turned into the spray, laughing as the cold sea hit my face—it felt like the best thing anyone had ever invented, and I knew Berlin was feeling it too, right beside me.
grounded_facts: Helena is on the prow of Ross's boat with Berlin.
A wave breaks and sprays her with seawater.
She reacts with visible joy, turning into the spray.
Her oilskins are soaked and her hair is streaming from the water.
She perceives that Berlin is also enjoying the moment.

memories.scene_title: 59 EXT. CAR PARK AT BEACH. DUSK.
memories.memory_text: I told Berlin that Ross had spoken about him—his age, his nervous tick, how he’s chubby—and when I said fifty-seven, he didn’t seem angry, just amused, like it was a game I wasn’t meant to know the rules to.
grounded_facts: HELENA reveals that Ross told her personal details about BERLIN: he is chubby, has a nervous tick, and is fifty-seven years old.
BERLIN responds with amusement rather than annoyance after HELENA reveals she knows his age.

memories.scene_title: 59 EXT. CAR PARK AT BEACH. DUSK.
memories.memory_text: The van came up fast behind us, jumping in and out of view with the dunes, and when it overtook, I knew—immediately—that it was the Hollow Car, the one that sounds hollow, and I said so, voice tight, before it vanished into the dark.
grounded_facts: A vehicle approaches quickly from behind, its headlights appearing and disappearing with the dune terrain.
HELENA identifies the overtaking vehicle as the 'Hollow Car' based on its sound and presence, expressing fear.

memories.memory_text: I reached for his hands, and for a moment I was so glad to see him—but then I felt it, each second stretching heavier, knowing this was the last time.
grounded_facts: Helena sees Berlin and is initially delighted.
She reaches for his hands.
She experiences increasing emotional weight with each passing second, realizing it is a final goodbye.

memories.memory_text: I wanted it to be a surprise, setting the table with the candles and the Forget-Me-Nots, waiting for him to come home.
grounded_facts: Helena prepared a surprise dinner for Berlin.
She set the table with candles, Forget-Me-Nots, and a can of Diet Coke.
She spoke the line 'I wanted it to be a surprise' as Berlin entered.

memories.memory_text: I knew before he said anything—he walked in too quiet, and the silence between us had already changed. When I asked if he wanted coffee, it wasn't just to fill the space, but to delay the moment just a little longer.
grounded_facts: Helena speaks before Berlin tells her he needs to talk, indicating she sensed his emotional state.
She references his silence since arriving and interprets it as 'thinking pretty loud'.
She initiates clearing the cups and offers to make more coffee after saying 'I know'.

memories.memory_text: I could feel the ache in my chest, standing there at the sink—knowing he was going to say goodbye, and not wanting to face it, not even to myself.
grounded_facts: Helena is alone in the kitchen after Berlin's departure.
She is aware of his impending goodbye before he speaks it.
Her facial expression reveals emotion only when she is unobserved.
She is missing him and feels an unexplained emotional pull.

memories.memory_text: I looked up from the sink and for just a second—there he was, a tall, strange face staring in, so close to the glass, and then gone.
grounded_facts: Helena sees a mysterious figure outside the kitchen window.
The figure is tall and has an unusual appearance.
She only sees him for an instant before he moves away quickly.
The encounter occurs while she is performing a routine task at the sink.

memories.scene_title: 76 EXT. (P.O.V. FROM FIRE ESCAPE) INSTITUTE. DUSK.
memories.memory_text: I remember handing him the forget-me-nots at the door, and how he took my hand before bringing it to his lips—then we kissed, clumsy and close, like we didn’t quite know how to say goodbye.
grounded_facts: Helena gives Berlin forget-me-nots at the door.
Berlin takes Helena's hand and kisses her fingertips.
They share a kiss that is described as awkward, with bumped noses.
The interaction occurs before they part and move into the hallway.

memories.memory_text: I closed the cupboard after stacking the last plate, and then I noticed something was off in the apartment—there was a sound I couldn’t place. I followed it to the bedroom and saw the curtains moving, fluttering in the dark, and realized the window was half open, though I didn’t remember opening it.
grounded_facts: Helena finished the dishes and put away the last plate.
She noticed an unfamiliar sound in the apartment.
She went into the bedroom and observed the curtains moving.
She discovered the window was half open.
She does not recall leaving the window open.

memories.memory_text: I checked the water temperature and turned the tap on when it was too cold. I silenced the indicator. Then I turned and saw someone in the dark.
grounded_facts: HELENA checks water temperature
HELENA turns on a tap
HELENA silences the liquid-level indicator
HELENA sees someone in the dark after silencing the indicator

memories.memory_text: A flash—dazzling light hit me, and I turned, certain I’d heard something, but there was only darkness and the feeling of being watched, then nothing.
grounded_facts: HELENA is struck by dazzling light from the intruder's camera flash
She twists in panic, reacting to a sound or sensation
She vocalizes fear but does not complete a scream
She hits at the darkness and the intruder is gone

memories.scene_title: 88 INT. LIVING ROOM. BERLIN'S HOUSE. DAY.
memories.memory_text: I was on the phone with Mr. Goodridge, and I could feel my voice shaking as I said I couldn't find the number—he kept pressing, and I just repeated that I’d call tomorrow, even though I already knew I wouldn’t.
grounded_facts: HELENA was on the phone and claimed she couldn't find her aunt's number.
She told Mr. Goodridge she would call him tomorrow with the number.
She appeared anxious and guilty during and after the call.
She sought Berlin's guidance before responding to Mr. Goodridge's request.

memories.memory_text: I remember the warmth of Berlin’s lips in the moonlight, how the music wrapped around us like it was the only thing breathing in the room.
grounded_facts: The scene takes place at night in a bedroom with moonlight coming through the windows.
HELENA and Berlin are silhouetted and begin kissing, described as 'a big close of lips meeting in moonlight'.
The kiss is prolonged and intense, with the narrative emphasizing that 'nothing else is happening in the whole fucking universe'.
Puccini's music plays in the background, described as a 'sweet pulse'.

memories.memory_text: The phone rang while we were kissing. When it was answered, I heard Berlin say, 'I gotta tell you, Margie. You are Mrs Santa Claus.'
grounded_facts: The phone began ringing during a kiss.
The kissing continued while the phone rang.
The phone was eventually answered.
Berlin spoke to someone named Margie.
Berlin said, 'I gotta tell you, Margie. You are Mrs Santa Claus.'
Berlin sounded pleased upon answering.

memories.scene_title: 93 INT. BEDROOM. THE ROSS RESIDENCE. DAY.
memories.memory_text: I asked Margie when John divorced and what his ex-wife was like—Suzanne, a model who left him—and how it broke him, how he couldn’t handle losing her.
grounded_facts: Helena asked Margie when John divorced.
Helena asked what Suzanne, John's ex-wife, was like.
Margie described Suzanne as a semi-professional model who left John.
Margie said John crashed after the divorce and drove to San Diego repeatedly, drinking heavily.

memories.scene_title: 93 INT. BEDROOM. THE ROSS RESIDENCE. DAY.
memories.memory_text: I wondered if John would like me in the red dress, standing there in the heels Margie made me try, feeling unsure but wanting to know what he’d think.
grounded_facts: Helena tried on red satin high-heeled shoes despite not wearing heels since she was 16.
The shoes fit and the experiment was a success.
Margie brought out a sultry red dress she had worn only once.
Helena asked Margie if John would like the dress on her.

memories.memory_text: I felt the weight of the mascara brush on my lashes, the drag of lipstick pulled carefully across my mouth—each stroke a step I couldn’t take back, standing there while MARGIE adjusted the red sequins over my shoulders.
grounded_facts: Helena applied bold makeup including bright red lipstick and jet black mascara.
Helena wore a dress made of blood red sequins.
MARGIE was present and involved in bringing Helena into the room.

memories.memory_text: I saw Ross’s eyes widen when he turned—his mouth opened before he said it—'Wow'—and for a second, I wasn’t sure if it was mockery or real, but the word hung there like it meant something.
grounded_facts: Ross reacted to Helena's appearance with an audible 'Wow'.
Helena entered the den with MARGIE, drawing immediate attention.
Helena perceived Ross's reaction as notable and possibly significant.

memories.memory_text: I said I couldn't walk in these shoes, and my voice sounded small, like it was coming from somewhere far away.
grounded_facts: Helena speaks the line 'I can't walk in these shoes' while sitting on the edge of a bed, visibly distressed.
She is wearing shoes that symbolize discomfort, possibly linked to emotional strain.

memories.memory_text: I felt his hands on my legs, the clip of my stockings releasing, and when he kissed me, I helped him with my brassiere—like I was both there and not.
grounded_facts: Berlin touches Helena's legs, unclips her stockings, and begins undressing her.
Helena reciprocates physical intimacy by helping remove her brassiere after responding to his kisses.

memories.memory_text: I remember the weight shifting on the bed, and then the silence where his breath used to be—just the wind, and something metallic clicking, loud, like it was inside my head.
grounded_facts: Helena was asleep in Berlin's arms at the start of the scene.
Berlin kissed her and left the bed while she was still asleep.
The sound of the gun being loaded was amplified in the scene's presentation.
Christmas carols were faintly audible in the background.

memories.memory_text: I saw my own body on the wall, naked in the bathroom, blown up like some trophy among all those other girls—he must have watched me without me knowing.
grounded_facts: A photograph of Helena standing naked in her bathroom is displayed as a large enlargement on the wall in the Myopic's attic apartment.
The photograph is part of a collage that includes images of half-dressed girls and is situated in a room filled with photographic equipment and pornographic magazines.
Helena is not present in the scene as it unfolds, but later learns or sees evidence of the photo's display, which aligns with her remembering the violation.

memories.scene_title: 121 EXT/INT. THE ROSS RESIDENCE. LIVING ROOM. DAY.
memories.memory_text: I remember asking John why I couldn't stay with him, my voice quiet but insistent, holding on to his hand like it was the only solid thing left.
grounded_facts: HELENA asks Berlin 'Why can't I stay with you?' twice in succession
HELENA is described as 'sallow for want of sleep and glad to have BERLIN's hand to hang on to'
Berlin responds by saying it's not possible and asks her not to ask questions now

memories.scene_title: 121 EXT/INT. THE ROSS RESIDENCE. LIVING ROOM. DAY.
memories.memory_text: I said I was going back to the institute, even after John told me no—not now—and Margie offered to have me stay with her instead.
grounded_facts: HELENA states 'I'm going back to the institute.'
BERLIN responds with 'No .. not now ..'
MARGIE offers for HELENA to stay at the house and look after her

memories.scene_title: 132 INT. KITCHEN. THE ROSS RESIDENCE. DAY.
memories.memory_text: I whispered, 'I need the number for a cab, please,' my fingers still touching the phone book, the silence after the words feeling heavier than before.
grounded_facts: Helena whispered a request for a cab number.
She was in physical contact with the phone book.
The request was made during a quiet moment in the kitchen.

memories.scene_title: 138 INT. LIVING ROOM/PORCH/FRONT YARD. HOUSE. DAY.
memories.memory_text: I stepped onto the porch and called out, 'John? Is that you?'—the fog was thick, and the only answer was the rattle of crows, nothing more.
grounded_facts: HELENA opened the door, thinking the visitor might be Berlin.
She stepped onto the porch and spoke into the quiet, asking if John was there.
The environment was silent except for distant natural sounds like crows and a bull.

memories.scene_title: 138 INT. LIVING ROOM/PORCH/FRONT YARD. HOUSE. DAY.
memories.memory_text: I bumped into a brown Chevrolet I hadn’t seen before, checked it quickly, and then froze—something clattered, maybe in the garage, and I asked again, 'John, is it you?' not knowing where the sound came from.
grounded_facts: HELENA physically encountered an unattended brown Chevrolet upon descending the stairs.
She explored the car and found nothing notable.
A sudden clattering sound caused her to become alert and question its source.
She called out a second time, directing the question toward a possible presence.

memories.memory_text: I felt my fingers brush the wardrobe door as I passed, and in that instant, the mirror showed someone behind me—completely in black, a man I hadn’t heard, hadn’t known was there—then his gloved hand clamped down on my wrist like a trap.
grounded_facts: HELENA touched the wardrobe as she passed it
The wardrobe mirror briefly revealed the intruder's reflection
The intruder grabbed her wrist with a gloved hand
She was shocked and speechless upon being grabbed

memories.memory_text: He took my hand and ran the blade over my palm, then closed my fingers around the handle. He said he cut my friend's head off with it. I couldn't move. Tears fell down my face.
grounded_facts: The intruder produced a stainless steel knife
He ran the edge of the blade over HELENA's palm
He closed HELENA's fingers around the knife handle
HELENA cried silently while holding the knife
The intruder claimed he used the knife to cut her friend's head off

memories.scene_title: 10 EXT. ENTRANCE/TRACK/INFILL. CITY DUMP. EUREKA. DAY.
memories.memory_text: I was already walking toward the infill when Travis told me the guy from L.A. had gone down there ahead of me—waiting for me, like he had any right to be on my dump, poking around before I even arrived.
grounded_facts: ROSS arrives at the city dump and begins walking toward the infill area.
Travis informs ROSS that the man from L.A. (Berlin) had been waiting and went down to the crime scene ahead of ROSS.
ROSS reacts with irritation, questioning why the man is there without authorization.

memories.scene_title: 11 EXT. WORKING FACE. INFILL. GARBAGE DUMP. DAY.
memories.memory_text: I saw the blood on the raincoat and heard Trimble say they cut his throat—knew right away it wasn't a clean suicide, not with that kind of cut.
grounded_facts: ROSS hears TRIMBLE state 'They cut his throat.'
The body is partially visible with a blood-stained raincoat.
ROSS reacts immediately to the nature of the wound, indicating recognition of violence beyond self-infliction.

memories.scene_title: 11 EXT. WORKING FACE. INFILL. GARBAGE DUMP. DAY.
memories.memory_text: I asked Berlin where the knife was—no weapon at a scene like that means someone took it, probably that kid hanging around who saw more than he let on.
grounded_facts: ROSS asks 'So where's the knife?' after assessing the scene.
BERLIN states he cannot find the knife and speculates the dozer might have moved it, but the weapon's absence is unexplained.
ROSS connects the missing knife to potential witness interference, implied by his focus on possession and distrust of official accounts.

memories.memory_text: I crouched in the garbage, shone my light into the sack, and saw it—a severed hand. I said, 'You're right. It's a fucken hand,' then handed the flashlight over.
grounded_facts: ROSS is crouching in garbage at the dump.
ROSS uses a flashlight to look inside a trash sack.
ROSS confirms the presence of a severed human hand.
ROSS speaks the line: 'You're right. It's a fucken hand.'
ROSS passes the flashlight to BERLIN.

memories.memory_text: I was soaked and freezing, scanning the trash under that goddamn rubber cape, when someone shouted about a brassiere with blood on it. All I could say was 'Oh, shit.'
grounded_facts: ROSS is present at the garbage dump during rainy, miserable conditions
ROSS hears a shout from VENABLES about discovering a bloodied brassiere
ROSS reacts verbally with 'Oh, shit.'

memories.memory_text: After Berlin said it was good to be with me, I cracked that I’d rather lie down with the derelict than stay another minute—and for once, he almost smiled. Then I told him, 'Glad you finally made it, Bro.'
grounded_facts: ROSS makes a darkly humorous comment about lying down with the derelict to escape the conditions
BERLIN responds with 'It's good to be with you, Ross.'
ROSS observes Berlin's near-smile and responds with 'Glad you finally made it, Bro.'
ROSS experiences a rare moment of visible happiness

memories.scene_title: 17 INT. KITCHEN. THE ROSS RESIDENCE. EUREKA. DUSK.
memories.memory_text: I pulled out the champagne, ready to celebrate, but Margie shot me down—said it was Californian, not French, like I claimed, and suddenly even that small thing felt like another thing I couldn't get right.
grounded_facts: ROSS retrieves a bottle of champagne from the refrigerator.
MARGIE corrects ROSS, stating the champagne is Californian, not French.
ROSS attempts to pour a drink, but BERLIN declines, and MARGIE offers BERLIN a diet soda instead.

memories.scene_title: 20 INT. CRIME LAB. POLICE STATION. DAWN.
memories.memory_text: I told Berlin to watch my lips and not get into it—shut him down hard when he started asking about 'frozen' and digging into the hand. That case wasn't ours, never was, and I wasn't gonna let him drag it back to life.
grounded_facts: ROSS enters the crime lab at 7:35 a.m. and sees BERLIN working on a severed hand.
BERLIN asks why ROSS is 'so coy about the word frozen'.
ROSS responds with 'Because, don't get into it...' and then says 'Watch my lips .. Don't get into it ..'.
ROSS states the case 'wasn't our case, wasn't our county, and got nothing to do with that'.

memories.scene_title: 21 INT. CRIME LAB. POLICE STATION. DAY.
memories.memory_text: Berlin asked about the 'Jennifer' case, and I told him it was an unsolved mess—half a million down the drain, and Taylor’s the one who worked it. I didn’t say it straight, but he knows Taylor blames him for the promotion.
grounded_facts: ROSS states the 'Jennifer' case is an 'unsolved' and mentions $500,000 was spent with no results.
ROSS directs BERLIN to TAYLOR, who worked the case, and implies TAYLOR resents BERLIN for stealing his promotion.
ROSS responds to BERLIN's inquiry about 'Jennifer' with skepticism and deflects further engagement.

memories.memory_text: I told Berlin about waking up with my heart pounding so hard I could've made love with my left tit — same as him, back when I was chained to cigarettes.
grounded_facts: ROSS shares a personal memory of struggling with smoking, including physical symptoms like a racing heart at night.
ROSS uses a vivid, sardonic metaphor to describe his past condition.

memories.memory_text: When he said a dollar wasn't worth quitting for, I upped it to fifty — and he took the bet, just like that.
grounded_facts: ROSS increases the bet from one dollar to fifty dollars to challenge Berlin's commitment to quitting smoking.
Berlin accepts the fifty-dollar bet immediately after hearing the amount.

memories.memory_text: I looked up from the papers and caught Citrine passing by—smoke in the air, sleeves rolled—and said I wanted to give that fat lady a lie-detector test, how she’s making my life a misery.
grounded_facts: ROSS is in the police station at night, going through documents with Detective Serato.
ROSS sees Citrine walking past while a teletype transmission is running.
ROSS interrupts Citrine to request a lie-detector test on the 'fat lady,' citing personal distress.

memories.scene_title: 26 INT. MERCEDES SEDAN. CITY OF EUREKA. NIGHT.
memories.memory_text: I asked Berlin what he thought the case was, and when he started obsessing over the Zippo again, I called him out—told him he couldn't stop clicking, picking, worrying. Same as back in L.A.
grounded_facts: ROSS asks Berlin what he thinks the case is.
ROSS observes Berlin fidgeting with the Zippo lighter.
ROSS confronts Berlin about his compulsive behavior, comparing it to his past in Los Angeles.

memories.scene_title: 26 INT. MERCEDES SEDAN. CITY OF EUREKA. NIGHT.
memories.memory_text: Berlin suddenly sprinted through the rain. He shouted that the victim was blind—because of the beeping traffic light and Braille on her fingertips.
grounded_facts: BERLIN suddenly exits the car and runs toward ROSS in the rain.
BERLIN announces his realization that the victim was blind and that she read Braille, connecting it to the beeping traffic light and marks on her fingertips.

memories.memory_text: My Chevy tore up the valley, sunlight flashing off the snow before the bend swallowed me, and I aimed it at that dead-eyed Victorian thing with fifty windows and a clock tower—like some goddamn asylum no one told me about.
grounded_facts: ROSS is driving a Chevy through a snowy valley in Trinity Forest.
The landscape is mountainous with juniper woods and fresh snow.
ROSS sees a distant, imposing building with a clock tower and many windows.
The car moves toward the building and disappears from view.

memories.memory_text: I remember standing there, ready to punch that smug look off Goodridge’s face when he acted like Amber just walking out was no big deal — a blind girl leaving with some unknown guy and he’s giving us lectures about independence.
grounded_facts: ROSS expressed frustration toward Principal Goodridge during questioning about Amber's departure.
ROSS challenged Goodridge on how a visually impaired student could leave with an unknown person without oversight.
ROSS physically reacted with aggression (urge to 'sock him in the crop') but was interrupted by a phone call.
Goodridge emphasized that Amber had useful vision and chose to leave independently.

memories.memory_text: I mouthed 'F.I.F.T.Y' slow and clear, watching Berlin stiffen without turning—Helena walking right through it, clueless, while he pretended the whole thing wasn't happening.
grounded_facts: ROSS silently mouths 'F.I.F.T.Y' to BERLIN in the corridor
HELENA is present but unaware of the pantomime
BERLIN does not respond visibly to the taunt

memories.memory_text: I leaned in close and whispered it straight into his ear—'Fifty fucken dollars, O.K.'—then followed up right after with 'Fifty of em. And I want em now,' just as the elevator doors opened.
grounded_facts: ROSS whispers 'Fifty fucken dollars, O.K.' into BERLIN's ear
ROSS immediately follows with 'Fifty of em. And I want em now'
The elevator arrives and doors open during the exchange

memories.scene_title: 39 EXT. HARBOR. HUMBOLDT BAY. EUREKA. DAY.
memories.memory_text: I was elbow-deep in the engine, trying to keep the old boat running, when Berlin started yelling over the noise about a ballistics match in San Diego—like that was supposed to mean something to me while I'm covered in oil and my son's watching the revs.
grounded_facts: ROSS is repairing the engine of his boat when BERLIN approaches.
The engine is loud, requiring BERLIN to shout.
ROSS briefly looks away to instruct his son about the revs.
ROSS signals BOBBY to turn off the diesel engine after BERLIN insists the matter is important.

memories.memory_text: I asked Berlin why the FBI didn’t put the cases together, since they worked on 'Jennifer' for months.
grounded_facts: ROSS questioned why the FBI did not connect the cases despite working on 'Jennifer' for months.
ROSS expressed skepticism about the FBI's failure to identify a pattern in the cases.

memories.memory_text: I told Berlin no—flat out. He wants to tear apart the Blind Institute, but walking in with his homicide and my mayor scandal? We’d lose everything. I couldn’t back him, even if part of me wanted to.
grounded_facts: ROSS refused to accompany Berlin to see Citrine.
ROSS cited political risk involving the mayor's associate as a reason for not supporting Berlin’s plan.
ROSS expressed internal conflict, acknowledging he didn’t enjoy saying no.

memories.scene_title: 52 INT. CRIME LAB. POLICE STATION. DAY.
memories.memory_text: I was just trying to keep my head down, pouring a drink, and then Serato walks in saying Emerson passed the polygraph with flying colors—like it was nothing. I remember the words hitting me: 'Oh my God Mother's shit. Are we in it?'
grounded_facts: ROSS is in the crime lab during the day, surrounded by labeled evidence in plastic sacks.
ROSS pours himself Scotch into a plastic cup and is present during a conversation with BERLIN and SERATO.
SERATO enters and states that someone (implied to be Emerson) passed the polygraph 'flying colors'.
ROSS responds with shock: 'Oh my God Mother's shit. Are we in it?'

memories.scene_title: 62 EXT. CITY STREET. CITY OF EUREKA. NIGHT.
memories.memory_text: I remember stepping out of the mist with Berlin, the cheap Christmas lights glowing overhead, and when he started whining about God, I shut him down—no God, just asses at kicking height.
grounded_facts: ROSS and BERLIN appear emerging from mist on a city street at night.
There are colored Christmas lights strung across the street.
BERLIN expresses frustration, saying 'God, it pisses me off, Ross.'
ROSS responds with a cynical remark denying the existence of God and making a darkly humorous comment about human anatomy.

memories.memory_text: I told Berlin he's dragging L.A. around like an addiction, just like I used to—pissed off and running on fumes, living off the vitamins in cigarettes. If there's a body under the snow, fuck it, let someone else dig it up.
grounded_facts: ROSS references his past anger and burnout, comparing himself to Berlin's current state
ROSS mentions living off 'the vitamins in cigarettes' and being 'up to my asshole in anger'
ROSS uses the metaphor of a body under the snow to dismiss the urgency of the case

memories.memory_text: I waved my empty glass at the bar, asking for another drink while telling Berlin he's getting too far into the element—she's blind, lonely, and he's a nice guy to have around, but emotional involvement screws everything up.
grounded_facts: ROSS signals for another drink by waving his empty glass
ROSS questions the credibility of the blind girl's testimony
ROSS warns Berlin about emotional involvement, implying it clouds judgment
ROSS refers to Berlin's potential 'nervous tick' as a consequence of stress

memories.scene_title: 86 INT. CRIME LAB. POLICE STATION. DAY.
memories.memory_text: I asked Berlin who he thought did it, even though he gave me that look like I was an idiot for asking.
grounded_facts: ROSS asks Berlin, 'Who d'you think it was?'
Berlin responds with a dismissive smile and calls the question stupid
ROSS insists, 'I'm asking it.'

memories.scene_title: 86 INT. CRIME LAB. POLICE STATION. DAY.
memories.memory_text: After he went off about the bad man out there, I didn't know what to say—just asked if I could help, and he didn't even know what to do with that.
grounded_facts: BERLIN delivers an intense warning about a dangerous unknown man
ROSS breaks eye contact after the confrontation
ROSS says, 'I dunno what I can do to help you?'
BERLIN responds, 'I dunno what I'd do with your help.'

memories.scene_title: 92 INT/EXT. KITCHEN/REAR ENTRANCE. ROSS'S HOUSE. DAY.
memories.memory_text: I ripped a beer from the crate and handed it to Berlin, told him not to thank me—Margie’s the one who stocked the place.
grounded_facts: ROSS opens a beer from the delivered supplies.
ROSS redirects Berlin's gratitude to Margie.

memories.scene_title: 92 INT/EXT. KITCHEN/REAR ENTRANCE. ROSS'S HOUSE. DAY.
memories.memory_text: I grabbed a six-pack and tossed it to Berlin, told him to give it to some old guy in a cell and say happy Christmas from me.
grounded_facts: ROSS retrieves a six-pack from his supplies.
ROSS instructs Berlin to deliver the beer to a prisoner with a Christmas message.

memories.memory_text: I told Berlin his gut's getting soft and his body's falling apart—mine's no better, gut in its fifties, balls in their sixties, feet in the eighties. Joked about it like it was nothing, even though the cold and the booze make every ache feel real.
grounded_facts: ROSS makes a darkly humorous comment about aging, referencing his own body parts and their supposed ages
ROSS contrasts his physical deterioration with Berlin's, using sarcasm to deflect discomfort
The dialogue occurs while both characters are in a cold sedan, drinking, during a long stakeout

memories.memory_text: Berlin snapped at me to hit the wipers—saw a light, thought it was a flashlight on the fourth floor. The second he said it, I was locked in, adrenalin kicking, everything suddenly loud and fast after all that stillness.
grounded_facts: BERLIN urgently instructs ROSS to activate the windshield wipers
ROSS responds to the command and clears the windshield
BERLIN reports seeing a flashlight moving across windows on the fourth floor of the institute
The atmosphere shifts abruptly from stagnation to high tension and action

memories.memory_text: I'm huddled against the wall, sleet slashing sideways, and the damn flashlight gives out—glows like a dying tangerine before going dark. Right when I'm trying to check Helena's place and make sure Berlin's alright, the light dies, and all I can hear is this noise upstairs, something moving.
grounded_facts: ROSS is positioned against a wall for shelter
sleet is moving nearly horizontally
ROSS's flashlight has stopped working and had a tangerine-colored glow
ROSS attempts to monitor Helena's apartment
ROSS expresses concern about Berlin's well-being
ROSS hears an indistinct sound from upstairs

memories.scene_title: 112 EXT. REAR OF INSTITUTE/FIRE ESCAPE. NIGHT.
memories.memory_text: I was getting torn apart by the wind, batteries almost dead, and still no answer from Berlin on the radio—tried every channel, kept asking if he heard me, but nothing. So I said fuck it, light or no light, I'm going up.
grounded_facts: ROSS is exposed to strong wind and darkness, increasing his anxiety.
ROSS repeatedly attempts to contact Berlin via radio on multiple channels without response.
ROSS decides to ascend the fire escape despite lack of communication and poor conditions.

memories.scene_title: 112 EXT. REAR OF INSTITUTE/FIRE ESCAPE. NIGHT.
memories.memory_text: A blinding flashlight hits me in the face from someone rushing down, I call out it's me, Freddy, but then I see the glint of a small gun at the edge of my vision—next thing I know, two shots tear into me, one nearly taking my hand off, the other slamming me in the gut.
grounded_facts: A figure in black descends the fire escape and shines a powerful flashlight directly into ROSS's face.
ROSS identifies himself verbally and pleads not to be shot.
ROSS sees a glint from a .25 Walther before being shot twice, with injuries to his hand and abdomen.

memories.scene_title: 115 EXT. REAR OF INSTITUTE. FIRE ESCAPE. NIGHT.
memories.memory_text: I tried to speak, but the blood filled my throat—nothing came out, just heaving, like an old bull elephant choking on its last breath.
grounded_facts: ROSS is mortally wounded by a gunshot to the throat.
ROSS attempts to speak but is physically unable due to the injury.
ROSS is conscious but on the verge of unconsciousness, experiencing labored breathing.
The scene includes sensory details of blood, cold, and muffled sound.
